narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic performance and test scores to predict first-time success. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, LSAT score of 30, full-time student status, and school decile rankings from early years—and calculated a 90% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. The decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers and strongly pushed the prediction toward failure, far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 2, another low ranking compared to the average around the sixth decile, which further reinforced the model's concern about your early academic trajectory. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, noticeably lower than the dataset average of 3.21, also contributed by nudging the prediction toward failure, though less forcefully than your deciles. These three academic performance indicators were the top drivers, outweighing factors like your family income bracket or full-time enrollment status, which is common for over 90% of students. In contrast to typical admitted students who boast stronger deciles, higher GPAs around 3.21, and LSATs near 37, your metrics signaled higher risk to the model. While your LSAT of 30 is below average, it wasn't the dominant factor here. This isn't a judgment on your potential or character, but a data-driven flag based on patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and targeting schools with more holistic reviews that might weigh your full-time commitment and background favorably. With targeted improvements, you can reshape your profile and reapply stronger next cycle.
